next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The dean's office was cold in the kind of mean way only school buildings managed in June, as if the air-conditioning existed less to cool anybody than to remind kids which rooms belonged to adults. Micah sat under a row of faded college pennants with his knees open and his jaw loose like none of this mattered. He kept his backpack hooked around one sneaker so the shaking in his leg did not travel into the whole chair.

Across from him, the trophy case shone with old football helmets and dust trapped behind glass. Every time the outer door opened, a rectangle of heat fell across the linoleum, then got swallowed by the office chill. Kids passed the doorway in clumps, loud and alive and headed somewhere that was not here. A couple of them looked in. Micah lifted his chin at them like he had chosen the chair and the wait and the white envelope clipped under his mother's thumb.

Marcia sat beside him in a navy blouse already darkened under the arms from the walk over. She had changed out of her church skirt and into work pants, but she still looked arranged, every button where it belonged, every edge under control. Her handbag sat upright on her lap. She was not digging through it, not checking her phone, not asking him again what happened. She had gone past talking.

That was worse than the talking would have been.

If she had wanted to cuss him out, he knew the shape of that weather. She would have gotten exact, then sharp, then loud enough to make the room understand she had not raised some wild little idiot for school people to pin to a bulletin board. Instead s...
open_hooks_to_carry: The labor yard shows Miami's racial and linguistic sorting system.
Delroy's pride is legible without making him noble by default.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ked paid. The handwriting looked thin and stubborn on the page.

Then she opened the drawer by the stove and took out the light bill.

The red band across the top shouted FINAL NOTICE in a font that was almost cheerful. She laid it beside the notebook on the table. Under the clear plastic, the edge of Tiana's housing form still showed if a person knew where to look. There were three papers there now. They held three separate futures demanding the same hand, the same wallet, the same Monday.

Outside, Delroy laughed at something on his phone, some man joke floating up warm through mosquito hum. From the bed area, Micah said, "Nah, he wish," and Tiana told him to shut up if he was going to keep lying that loudly. Their voices tangled, sharp and ordinary. It was family noise. It was life noise. It made the room sound held together when it was mostly held up.

Marcia stacked the notebook on top of the bill, then stood there with both palms resting on the covers as if she could press paper into mercy. The electric money was already in Yvette's hand. The code notice still sat folded in her purse. Micah's meeting was at 8:15. Tiana had hidden a college deadline under the table where people usually stored coupons and church flyers.

Sunday had not stayed ordinary. It had only dressed itself that way long enough to get through the door.

She slid the notebook back into the cupboard and left the light bill on the table where she would have to see it at dawn. July had to come now. She had paid for that future with Tuesday's current, and there was no getting either one back by wishing.
state_deltas_required: Find steady work before the housing notice becomes eviction in fact.
He borrows $400 from Claude and quietly commits himself to a future demolition job, beginning a debt he hides from Marcia.
